summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – Existence of de facto relationship – Where the applicant seeks a declaration that a de facto relationship lasting more than two years existed between the parties pursuant to s 90RD(1) of the Act – Where the applicant seeks a declaration that he made substantial contributions to the relationship pursuant to s 90RD(2)(c) – Where the parties’ are in conflict about most factual matters – Where the respondent’s evidence was treated with a great deal of circumspection – Where it was declared that a de facto relationship lasting more than two years existed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

In the context of a contested application for property adjustment between alleged former de facto spouses, on 25 June 2024 a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) ordered a “discrete hearing to determine the existence of a de facto relationship”. Before the discrete hearing was held, the application was transferred to this court.
The applicant seeks declarations pursuant to ss 90RD(1), (2)(a) and (2)(c) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) that a de facto relationship existed between him and the respondent between June 2018 and April 2023. Alternatively, in the event that the court considers that a de facto relationship existed, but was for less than two years, he seeks a declaration that he made substantial contributions of a kind mentioned in ss 90SM(4)(a), (b) or (c) of the Act. The respondent opposes the application and contends that the parties were never in a de facto relationship.
These reasons explain my conclusion that the parties were in a de facto relationship between July 2018 and April 2023 and why I have made the declaration set out at the commencement of these reasons.

De facto relationships for the purposes of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)

Two people who are not married to each other, nor related by family but who have a relationship as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis are in a de facto relationship for the purposes of the Act (s 4AA(1) of the Act). At issue in this proceeding is whether the parties lived together on a genuine domestic basis. They have never been married to each other, nor are they related by family.
Determining whether the parties lived together on a genuine domestic basis over the period or periods alleged requires an analysis of the facts of the case at hand guided, but not limited by, a consideration of the non-exhaustive list of circumstances that might inform such a decision set out in s 4AA(2) of the Act. “Consistently with the reality that human relationships are infinitely mutable, in determining whether a de facto relationship exists a court is entitled to have regard to such matters, and to attach such weight to any matter, as may seem appropriate” (Fairbairn v Radecki [2022] HCA 18; (2022) 275 CLR 400, at [28]).
In general terms, the applicant contends that the parties were in a de facto relationship from June 2018 to May 2022 and then from July 2022 to 6 April 2023. The respondent accepts that the parties were in a romantic relationship, but she says that it was very brief and that the parties’ relationship was really nothing more than a business relationship.
The applicant bears the onus of proving the existence of the de facto relationship. He must do so to the standard prescribed by s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), namely proof on the balance of probabilities. It is apparent from the respondent’s materials and her statements to me in court, that she considers the relevant standard is proof beyond a reasonable doubt. That submission is incorrect and I informed her of her error. Whether she accepted that guidance is unclear, although I am satisfied that she understood it.

A word about the evidence given by the parties

Counsel for the applicant submits that I should find that the applicant is both honest and reliable. She submits that his evidence is corroborated by independent evidence in various respects. I accept these submissions as a general proposition, but where it has been necessary to do so, I have examined all of the evidence, rather than rely upon general impressions of credibility. Whilst it is right to say that the applicant’s credit and the reliability of his evidence was not challenged in cross-examination, I have borne in mind that the self-represented respondent is not a lawyer and her cross-examination was informed by her oft repeated view that because she had sworn to her version of the facts, what she says must be true without question. Nonetheless, I accept the applicant’s submission that as a general proposition, his evidence should attract significant weight.
In her written submissions, counsel for the applicant characterises the respondent’s affidavits as having been cast from the mistaken viewpoint that she was defending herself in these proceedings, that she had the right to silence and the right not to give evidence which would “incriminate her”. I think that is an accurate characterisation. The respondent’s affidavits are more submission than evidence and in a great many areas, assume the correctness of the applicant’s evidence but seek to characterise the facts in way that leads to the result she desires.
I found the respondent a difficult witness. She seemed offended at the suggestion that she should be cross-examined and initially she refused to be cross-examined on the basis that she had sworn her evidence and it should therefore be accepted at face value and without question. She did not explain how I should consistently apply that approach to all the evidence in the case, including that of the applicant. Nonetheless, she eventually submitted to cross-examination. Much like her affidavits, her evidence in cross-examination was argumentative and defensive.
The way in which the respondent describes herself in her affidavits and the language and concepts she employs in them bear the hallmarks of someone who has enthusiastically engaged with writings on the “common lore”. Thus, parts of the respondent’s affidavits mis-state legal principles or the effect of those principles. There are quotes from the King James Bible (a rather large quarto sized version of which the respondent wished to take into the witness box when she was cross-examined). There are many references to the “contract” between she and the applicant, but there was no effort to prove any contract in the common law sense.
There are other passages which are, well, just bizarre. For example, counsel for the applicant highlighted these two paragraphs from the respondent’s affidavit of evidence-in-chief (faithfully reproduced, errors in the original):
99... We absolutely never slept together, he’s lying. What’s meant by “normality” I have no idea, it wasn’t normal, I got the impression he wanted to stay forever. Contract had ended, marching orders given, I wanted him gone and he’s ignoring it. I’m pissed off reading always suggesting sleeping together, in a romantic fairy-tale that ended 2020

100. Saving princess Cindarella August 2022, damsel in distress, Darth Vader coming to the rescue, police storm troopers arresting her, locking her up, Vader coming to the rescue liberating her is an absolute hypocrisy suggesting of an unrelated event; is of no relevance in establishing defacto.”
There are many others of a similar character.
I have some other things to say about the respondent’s case more generally towards the conclusion of these reasons, but presently, it is enough to record that I found that I must approach the respondent’s evidence and her submissions with a good deal of circumspection.

Background and facts

It is not in contest that the parties met in or around late 2017 or early 2018 at a store in Town B where the applicant was then working. The respondent was a regular customer. The applicant describes an approach to him from the respondent to see if he was “interested in her”. He describes receiving a note from the respondent making this inquiry.
For her part the respondent rejects the applicant’s version. She says that he pursued her and that his conduct was predatory and any meetings with him were not consensual. He made a pest of himself when she would go to the agency for supplies.
The applicant called evidence from Ms C, the respondent’s neighbour. She gives the following written evidence, that was unmolested by cross-examination:
[Ms Falvo] would attend my home at least twice a week. I recall in early 2018 [Ms Falvo] asked me how to approach a man called [Mr Melita] who she had met to ascertain if he was interested and had feelings about her. I suggested to [Ms Falvo] that she write a note to him explaining that she had the impression that he was flirting and interested in her and that she would be receptive to a friendship with him and possibly forming a relationship.
I prefer the applicant’s evidence about the parties’ initial meeting.
The parties are at odds about whether they met up in February and March 2018. The applicant’s evidence is that they did. The respondent swears in one of her affidavits (filed on 7 August 2024) that she did not pursue the applicant and that there were no meetings with him in February or March 2018. She describes the applicant’s evidence about such meetings as “pure fabrication”. These denials are repeated in her affidavit filed on 4 July 2025. However, in cross-examination the respondent accepted (reluctantly) that in an affidavit filed on 30 May 2024 she swore that she did meet with the applicant in March 2018.
I am satisfied that the parties organised a meeting at the end of February 2018 whereat they talked for some time. They continued to meet casually in March 2018. The applicant swears, and I accept, that during this time his affection for the respondent grew and, to his observation, hers grew for him. I accept the applicant’s evidence that they commenced a sexual relationship in late March 2018. I reject the respondent’s denials of these matters.
The applicant called evidence from Ms D. She and the applicant have a son, E, together. E lives with Ms D but has spent time with the applicant when it has been able to be arranged. In her affidavit filed on 20 June 2025, Ms D gives evidence that in April 2018 she visited the applicant with E. She saw “love notes written on little scraps of paper to” the applicant from the respondent “all around the house”. I accept this evidence. It is consistent with the applicant’s evidence about the development of the relationship between he and the respondent.
At this time, both parties were involved with tying up the loose ends of previous relationships: the applicant with his former de facto Ms F with whom he had ongoing court proceedings and the respondent with her former de facto, Mr G, with whom she too had ongoing legal proceedings.
The applicant lived at a property at Suburb H. The respondent lived at a property at Suburb J. Their residences were about half an hour’s drive apart. The respondent has three children, two of whom, Ms K and Mr L, lived with her at Suburb J.
The applicant swears and I accept, that over the period spanning April to June 2018 the respondent and her children spent increasing time at his Suburb H property.
According to the applicant’s evidence, the residence on the Suburb J property was very basic. It had no reticulated electricity and only one solar panel that powered a single light. There was tank water only, the property was generally overgrown with weeds and the fencing was in poor repair. The photographs annexed to the applicant’s trial affidavit certainly depict a modest dwelling.
The respondent, when asked about this in cross-examination, denied that the applicant’s description was accurate. She said that it was perfectly suited to her and her children’s needs. However, exhibit 8 is an affidavit that the respondent swore in her proceedings with Mr G on 16 February 2019. Her description of the dwelling at paragraphs 62 – 69 is entirely consistent with the applicant’s description. Her evidence before me about this dwelling was simply disingenuous and I reject it. Her evidence in the Mr G proceedings was plainly by way of complaint that living conditions that she and the children had to endure were substandard.
I am satisfied that the applicant and her children spent considerable time at the Suburb H property because of its greater amenity and because the applicant and the respondent were in the early stages of their romantic relationship. No doubt passions were high and they spent as much time with each other as they could. That observation is consistent with the evidence of Ms D and Ms C.
The respondent relied upon evidence from each of her children. I have an affidavit from Ms K filed on 19 June 2025. Ms K was not available for cross-examination and so her evidence needs to be considered in that light. The language, tone and content of her evidence demonstrates a partisanship that cannot be ignored. Whilst her evidence is strongly supportive of the respondent’s case and paints the applicant in an entirely negative light, parts of it are inconsistent with the respondent’s own evidence (as it has been from time to time). In the end, I have resolved to give Ms K’s evidence no weight.
I also have an affidavit from Mr L. Mr L was cross-examined. In the end, I do not think that his evidence carries much weight. His evidence, as demonstrated by cross-examination showed that it was tailored to suit the respondent and was plainly inconsistent with the observations of Mr M, a person with no interest in the outcome of these proceedings. It was also inconsistent with the respondent’s evidence that she gave in her parenting proceedings with Mr G. I have referred to these matters later in these reasons.
The applicant says that by July 2018 he had formed the view that his relationship with the respondent was serious and worth pursuing. I accept his evidence that by this time the parties were living between his property at Suburb H and the respondent’s property at Suburb J, but mostly at his property because it had better amenities than did the respondent’s. They spent many nights together at one property or the other. When the applicant stayed at the Suburb J property, he and the applicant slept in a tent on the veranda and the respondent’s children slept in the cottage.
I am satisfied that it was at about this point that the respondent gave the applicant a key to the lock on the front gate of the Suburb J property, which she always kept locked. The respondent had experienced a home invasion at a previous address and consequently she was careful to keep the gate to her property locked. It seems curious that she would give the applicant a key to the property in those circumstances if it truly was the case that there was nothing other than some sort of prospective business relationship between them as she now alleges. If that were the only reason for him having a key, it does not sit comfortably with the uncontested fact that the applicant did not move livestock onto the Suburb J property until sometime in 2019. The applicant kept this key until April 2023 when he says the parties finally separated.
During 2018, the applicant purchased a quantity (the evidence does not permit of a more precise finding) of bare rooted flowers and planted them in garden beds at the Suburb J property. Whilst the respondent initially denied this when cross-examined about it, when challenged with her sworn testimony contained in her affidavit filed on 30 May 2024 she recanted and accepted that the applicant had indeed purchased and planted a significant number of flower bushes for her.
The respondent was interested in textiles and fabric and made hand-sewn clothing. She, with the applicant’s support, planned to open a business, N Business in nearby Town B to sell her products. The flowers planted at Suburb J fitted into that plan. The parties intended to sell the blooms from the business.
In July 2018 the applicant helped the respondent collect some furniture, including a double bed, from Town O in Queensland. The double bed was placed on the mezzanine floor in the cottage on the Suburb J property and from that point the parties would sleep together in that double bed when they both stayed at Suburb J.
I accept the applicant’s evidence that by about August 2018 he and the respondent were spending most of the week together, mostly at Suburb H. Their sexual relationship continued. The applicant was at the Suburb J property on average about once per week and performed maintenance work to the property generally as well as establishing gardens.
The applicant was, I am satisfied, providing financial support to the respondent and her children. She had little by way of independent income and he provided cash for necessaries such as food, groceries and other living expenses. Not surprisingly, the applicant had no corroborative documents of this expenditure, but that does not mean he did not make it. Although I am unable to make a finding about the precise amounts involved, I am satisfied that he made financial provision as he claims.
At the end of August 2018, the applicant paid $14,896.00 for the installation of a solar electricity system at the respondent’s property. So much is not in dispute.
Why the applicant met that cost is in dispute. His case is that he offered to pay for the installation of the solar power system at the applicant’s property to improve its amenity for her, him and her children. The parties were, I am satisfied, in a romantic relationship and this was part of the consolidation and solidification of their romantic relationship.
The respondent alleges that the installation of the solar system was for the applicant’s “own purpose, for maintaining use of power tools/charging his batteries for his farm equipment, electric fencing, for the management of his herd of livestock soon to arrive on the farm on a business agreement for agisting”. But this is entirely inconsistent with her deposition on 16 February 2019 in the Mr G proceedings where she said:
At the time of [Mr G] leaving the house we had no power, no oven, no hot water, no refrigerator, no beds and no tables for meals or for the children to do their school work. [Mr G] had about 6 tables in his possession.
We have no oven for cooking. I use a portable camp gas bench top cooker and gas bottle inside.
For two years, the children and I had no refrigerator. We have used eskies and bought ice every second day. Sadly, food perished and had to be thrown out. It was time consuming and expensive to drive into town to frequently buy ice and small amounts of food. We now have a small bar fridge.
We have no hot water inside the house. The children and I have to boil water. In summer, we wash in the creek. In winter. we boil the water and take sponge baths in front of the fireplace. Eight months after we commenced living on the property I sold a horse float and set up an outdoor gas hot water shower system. We attach the small motor to some clips which we attach to the battery of the car.
66. The children and I read at night using torches and candles.

67. ...

68. Recently a friend gave me a loan so I could install a solar system. We now have two outlets for power.
On this evidence, installation of the solar system was both necessary for the comfort and safety of the respondent and her children and something which she either undertook herself or requested the applicant to do. Exhibit 14 is what appears to be a quote from the supplier/installer addressed to “[Mr Melita & Ms Falvo]”. The subsequent invoice dated 23 August 2018 is addressed in the same way. These documents are consistent with the applicant’s evidence and the respondent’s February 2019 evidence, but not her evidence before me.
Also part of exhibit 14 is what purports to be a written loan agreement, or perhaps an acknowledgment, signed by each of them and dated in late July 2018, which provides:
(a) the applicant will loan to the respondent $15,000 over time and by way of various unspecified payments on her behalf;

(b) the loan term is four years or until the Suburb J property is sold; and

(c) repayments of $100.00 per month were to be made by the respondent into the applicant’s nominated bank account, commencing July 2019.

The applicant says that the loan document came about at the respondent’s suggestion. He says that she told him that if it was termed a loan it would benefit her in her property settlement with Mr G. The applicant says that the respondent drafted the loan agreement. He also alleges, and she agrees, that she never made any of the repayments provided for in the document.
The respondent says that she felt awkward about the expense of the installation and she “executed a contract to pay him back for the installation”. More than that, she swears that the “installers arrived, solar panels were being installed without negotiating or our agreement final – he just forced it on us – we were perplexed about the whole situation, what was happening.” She then swears (respondent’s affidavit sworn 7 August 2024, page 22):
It felt like a whole new home invasion all over again: we're stunned, frozen in our tracks, PTSD all over again; struggling to deal with what's happening.

Now the panels were here; later I decide to make an agreement; an executed signed contract to pay for the panel installation in good faith. I didn't feel comfortable at the time accepting the solar panel installation forced upon us and though here, we needed to paying for them; our way of saying we pay our way independently.
Further, the respondent claims that she had an agreement with the applicant for him to agist livestock on her property and the agistment fees (unpaid) were set off against “the installation of the solar panel system in my mind”.
I prefer the applicant’s evidence about these matters. I accept his motivation for meeting this cost although I accept that it would also be of benefit to him to do the things the respondent alleges. But I do not consider that those reasons were any part of the motivating factor for his generosity. The respondent’s claims about the reasons for the solar installation (to suit the applicant’s business interests) do not fit the chronology and are entirely inconsistent with her February 2019 testimony. Moreover, he had no livestock on her property at that point and none arrived until 2019. The work he was doing directly benefited the respondent rather than advancing any business in which he was involved.
The respondent accepts that the applicant did express concern about how she and her children lived, although she now reframes his concern as part of a subterfuge on his part to swindle her out of her land. I reject her contention about that.
I accept the applicant’s evidence about the installation of the solar power system, including the reasons for its installation. I accept that the formulation and documenting of the installation costs as a loan was the respondent’s idea which she thought would bring her some advantage in her property settlement with Mr G. Indeed, the “loan” appears in the balance sheet contended for by the respondent in her affidavit filed on 16 February 2019 in the Mr G proceedings.
I find that between May and September 2018 the applicant purchased a refrigerator and a freezer and some lights for the Suburb J property. The respondent argues that there are no “permanent lights” at the Suburb J property, but the applicant does not allege that. In any event, she does not explain what she means by the expression “permanent lights”. I expect that the parties are talking about the same objects installed at the cottage, just describing them differently. The applicant also constructed two flyscreen doors and made screens for some windows. He assisted with the construction of a new veranda and paid the builder $14,000 for that construction. The respondent makes the point that the purchases are not vouched by documents, but I do not consider that is necessary. I accept the applicant’s evidence about these purchases.
The respondent took some of her possessions to the applicant’s Suburb H property. In particular, in September 2018 she took her sewing equipment and fabrics there and used the sunroom and entrance area of the home as a workspace with the applicant’s consent. The respondent denies that she ever did so, but the presence of the sewing equipment and fabrics in the Suburb H property was observed by Ms D. She gave evidence, which I accept, that she saw the equipment in a room at the property on one of her visits there when E was visiting the applicant.
The sewing equipment was to be used by the respondent for producing hand-sewn garments that she intended to sell at N Business. Having regard to the photographs in evidence, her evidence in her February 2019 affidavit and the applicant’s observations of the Suburb J property, I reject the respondent’s evidence that she had room to set up all of her sewing equipment at her property. The Suburb H property provided a greater area in which she could work.
The parties went camping together at Town P in October 2018, November 2019 and February 2020. On each occasion, they slept together in the same tent. The respondent’s children slept in single tents. The respondent says that she cannot remember this happening, but I accept the applicant’s evidence about these events.
The applicant says that by November or December 2018 he and the respondent were sharing their “daily lives together”. I accept that they were spending much of their free time together and were, in effect, living full-time together between Suburb J and Suburb H. The applicant says that the respondent would often drop into his workplace and bring him a snack at lunchtime or morning tea or just to say hello.
Although the respondent denies it, I accept the applicant’s evidence that together they shopped weekly for groceries, purchased clothing, had lunches in cafes and visited local beaches and attractions. They would plan shopping trips to City Q, Town R or Town P. The applicant says that the parties “were always holding hands and being affectionate”. I accept his evidence about that. That behaviour is consistent with the observations of the parties made by Ms C when they visited her.
I accept that the parties talked of matters of mutual concern including their respective property settlements and the parenting issues between the respondent and Mr G. They also discussed projects on the Suburb J property including building cabins to create an additional income stream. They talked about travel together and in particular travel to Country S where the respondent could pursue her interest in linen and other fabrics.
It is uncontentious that the respondent moved some livestock onto the Suburb J property in mid-2019. Why that came about is in dispute.
The applicant says that in 2019 he moved about eight head of livestock onto the Suburb J property at the respondent’s request “to keep the grass down”. He says that he had no particular need to put the livestock onto her property. He had, by then, sold most of the livestock that had been depastured on the Suburb H property and was agisting other livestock on another property in Suburb T. In any event, he says that at the respondent’s request he moved eight head onto Suburb J. He also set up a system to easily get water to the livestock by purchasing and installing a 24000-litre water tank, associated pump and lines to service both the livestock and the dwelling.
The respondent says this is all a fabrication. She says that she already had horses on the property and they looked after the grass. Other parts of the property were slashed. She says that this was nothing more than the applicant doing what he wanted to do and was part of some ill-defined business arrangement that she says she established with the applicant. However, I reject her evidence about these matters. Having regard to her evidence in the 16 February 2019 affidavit in the Mr G proceedings it seems tolerably clear that all of the horses on the property were gone by February 2019. I prefer the evidence of the applicant about why his livestock came to be moved to the Suburb J property.
It is uncontentious that the respondent conducted the N Business from about June 2019 until May 2020. I am satisfied that the applicant financed the start-up costs for this. Although the respondent denies that he did so, she does not identify any source of funds available to her from which she could have met the costs. According to the respondent’s own evidence in the Mr G proceedings, she had no capital from which to fund those costs. It must have been funded by the applicant and he had sources of income and capital from which to make provision for the respondent and the business.
Until this time, the parties continued to move between the Suburb H property and the Suburb J property. However, the applicant says that towards the end of July 2019 he and the respondent discussed him moving into the Suburb J property. He had sold the Suburb H property and it was due to settle at some point in the not-too-distant future. A decision was required because the applicant needed to move his farming equipment from Suburb H before settlement and needed to know where to move it. The applicant says that the respondent suggested that he should move his equipment to the Suburb J property.
The applicant says that he and the respondent advanced their discussions about building dwellings on the Suburb J property and that he should purchase shipping containers for storage. They planned to use the applicant’s funds liberated from the settlement of Suburb H to fund their building plans. However, the settlement of Suburb H was delayed for some time. Moreover, the applicant agreed that some of the funds liberated from that sale were to be used to allow the respondent to pay out Mr G in their property settlement so that she could receive the Suburb J property as hers.
At the same time, the applicant’s property settlement with his former partner was coming to a head and he says that he received an offer to resolve that matter which, although low from his point of view, he accepted. The parties discussed their respective legal cases with each other and offered and received words of advice and encouragement one from the other.
At about this time, the parties engaged Mr U, solicitor to act for them in their respective matrimonial disputes. The respondent called Mr U to give evidence and I have an affidavit by him filed on 18 June 2025. He was cross-examined.
Mr U said that in mid-2019 the parties informed him that they had been sacked by their former lawyer (who also acted for both of them) because they were in a relationship. He formed a “clear impression” when he first took instructions that the applicant and the respondent were a couple. He gave evidence that they were both invested in the other’s proceedings and the outcome of them. They attend joint appointments with Mr U where they allowed the other to hear all of their personal and financial information about their family law disputes and proceedings. He had no doubt that they were a de facto couple.
In about August 2019 the applicant commenced moving farming equipment from Suburb H to the Suburb J property. He purchased two shipping containers
The applicant swears that in August 2019 the respondent proposed that they should get engaged to be married. She said, according to the applicant, “I want to make this relationship official in the eyes of the mighty and this to be proper and wear your engagement ring, so people know we are together as a couple”. I am satisfied that the respondent said words to this effect. He accepted her proposition. The parties planned an engagement party, their wedding and which surname they would use after marriage.
From September 2019, the applicant and the respondent resided together, along with the respondent’s children Ms K and Mr L, exclusively at the Suburb J Property. The applicant continued to clear up things at the Suburb H property for its impending settlement. He completed moving his equipment to Suburb J by November 2019.
In September 2019 the respondent selected an engagement ring and wedding ring. The applicant purchased the rings and from their collection, which was likely sometime in October 2019, the respondent commenced wearing her engagement ring.
The parties had a party in December 2019 at the Suburb J property and invited friends from the local area, including Mr V and Ms C and people named Mr W and Ms X who no longer live in the area. The applicant says it was an engagement party, the respondent says it was nothing of the sort, but rather an end of year gathering. She denied there was any announcement of the parties’ engagement.
The applicant put into evidence photographs of the engagement party showing their friends and Ms K and Mr L in attendance. There are photographs of the respondent sitting on the applicant’s lap showing off the engagement ring. Her explanation for this photo and her evidence about this event more generally was simply disingenuous.
Ms C gave evidence of her observations at the party. She says:
My husband and I were asked by [Ms Falvo] to a small gathering held in the [garden] of the cottage at [Suburb J]. In attendance were our neighbours [Ms X] and [Mr W], [Ms Falvo], [Mr Melita] and two of [Ms Falvo’s] children, [Mr L] and [Ms K].
This gathering occurred in December of 2019 at which I recall [Ms Falvo] and [Mr Melita] both stood up together at the end of a trestle table that was being used for food and nibbles. I recall [Ms Falvo] then held up her hand to show everyone the engagement ring.
[Ms Falvo] had a diamond ring given to her by [Mr Melvita], and she told me how she helped to choose it.
[Ms Falvo] stated to me that a wedding date was set for [May 2020] and the ceremony was to be held on a [beach] at [Town P] and that my husband and I would be in attendance, followed by a meal at her favourite [Restaurant] in [Town R].
This evidence was untroubled by any successful challenge in cross-examination.
I accept that the parties became engaged as the applicant claims, that he purchased an engagement ring for the respondent and they held a small engagement party in December 2019 to announce their engagement to those at the party. I would have reached this conclusion even without reference to the respondent’s inconsistent statements in her affidavit filed on 7 August 2024 to the effect that the engagement was terminated early to mid-2020. If the parties were not engaged, there would be nothing to terminate.
The parties planned for their wedding to take place in 2020 at, the applicant says, a beach in Town P with the reception to be held at a restaurant in Town R. This is consistent with what Ms C says the respondent told her in late 2019. The applicant put into evidence a photograph of what he says is a whiteboard kept by the respondent at the N Business shop depicting the word “Married” in May 2020.
At some point in January 2020, the applicant reported the parties’ relationship to Centrelink. Exhibit 4 is a copy of the form which was lodged with Centrelink in January 2020 showing that he had a de facto partner, although he does not appear to have named her. His address is given as the Suburb J property.
The applicant says that during the summer months in early 2020 he worked on the Suburb J property in the garden and was building a shed in which he installed a shower and bath. He says that he and the respondent enjoyed romantic swims at a local waterhole and would have regular “intimate times” at the creek.
By this time the respondent had opened her business in Town B. It had a social media page and Ms D was a follower. She gives evidence of a post made on the page (by the account holder presumably) of a photograph of some wine, a charcuterie board and some French fries with the accompanying caption, “[N Business] Oh yum ... Afternoon indulgence ... for me and my gorgeous man today ... treat yourself xxx”. The respondent was cross examined about this post and despite her tortuous answers, I am satisfied that the reference to “my gorgeous man” was a reference to the applicant. A curious post indeed if the respondent and the applicant were not in a relationship, not engaged and only had business relationship involving the agistment of livestock.
However, it was not all bucolic bliss, intimate times down the creek and wine and charcuterie in the business. The applicant says that the respondent often reacted angrily to minor matters that he considered did not warrant an angry response from her. One such occasion that resulted in an argument occurred on the day before the respondent’s birthday in 2020. The subject matter of the dispute is unimportant, but the following day, the applicant offered to take the respondent and Mr L to a local cafe to celebrate her birthday. She refused and instead went out with Ms K and Mr L without him. According to the applicant somewhat uncharacteristically, the respondent, some days later, apologised for her behaviour.
There was another occasion where harsh words were exchanged between the parties and the respondent alleges that the applicant hit her somehow with a brush cutter, but I reject that allegation. I prefer the applicant’s evidence about this incident.
There was another argument about which the applicant gave evidence arising from his expectation that he might eat with the rest of the family, only to be told to wait until they had finished, although the time frame for this is not clear.
In March 2020 COVID-19 restrictions commenced and the parties cancelled their immediate wedding plans for May 2020. The respondent also cancelled a planned trip for her and Ms K to travel to Country S for Ms K’s birthday, during which time the applicant was going to care for Mr L.
Because the applicant considered that he was in a long-term relationship with the respondent, he says that he changed his postal address for matters such as his banking accounts, to the Suburb J address.
In early 2020 the parties organised for some building work to be carried out at the Suburb J property. I have evidence from the builder, Mr M. He says that he first met the parties early in 2020 when there was discussion about him building a deck or veranda and then some cabins at the property. His description of the property in early 2020 is consistent with the findings I have made earlier about its condition. He describes the respondent as directing the conversation about the work to be done on the property.
Mr M confirmed that he completed the veranda restoration work between March and April 2020. He says that the applicant paid him $14,000. While he was doing the work, he would arrive at the property between 7.00am and 9.00am. He says he was on site for about 8 to 10 hours per day. On occasions when he arrived, the applicant and the respondent would still be in bed in the loft of the cottage. Although he did not see them directly together in bed, he did see them emerge and drew that conclusion. The children would either be up or still on bed. He says that the respondent directed the work in the main and he observed the applicant with Mr L often in the garden or working on other farm jobs. He thought that “[Mr Melita] was great with [Mr L], from what I saw and heard [Mr Melita] was supportive, showing [Mr L] ways to do tasks and [Mr L] reciprocating his willingness to participate, he was by [Mr Melita’s] side engaged in tasks and interacting”. I accept Mr M’s evidence about these matters.
In March 2020 the respondent made a recording of the parties fixing a fence on the Suburb J property. The language and tone used by the respondent in that recording is consistent with the parties having a warm, intimate relationship.
In June 2020, the applicant attended the interviews for a family report that was prepared for the purposes of the respondent’s parenting proceedings with Mr G. That seems a very curious thing indeed, if the applicant’s involvement with the respondent and her children was as she describes in her evidence (and Mr L’s evidence), a business relationship. The respondent’s attempts to explain this evidence in cross-examination were unpersuasive.
Indeed, on 2 June 2020 the respondent swore an affidavit for use in her proceedings with Mr G in which she described the applicant as her “partner”. She describes the applicant’s relationship with both Mr L and Ms K as “very positive” (contrary to that which is now sworn to by Mr L and Ms K). The affidavit was drawn on instructions by Mr U. So too, the respondent’s attempts to explain this evidence in cross-examination were unpersuasive.
In August 2020 Town B Police attended the Suburb J property to discuss certain matters with the respondent. Neither she nor the applicant was home. The police climbed over the locked gate that displayed 'no trespassing' signs and were met by Ms K. After a short discussion they left.
The respondent sued the New South Wales police for trespass. The applicant says that the case consumed a significant amount of the respondent’s time, as one would expect. It was ultimately successful: Falvo v State of New South Wales [2023] NSWSC [...].
Annexed to Mr M’s affidavit are some text messages sent from the respondent to Mr M about the cabins to be built at Suburb J. Some of them are signed off by the respondent sing the words “[Mr Melita] and [Ms Falvo]”.
In November 2020 the respondent swore another affidavit in her proceedings with Mr G. In that affidavit she describes the applicant as “my current partner” and sets out her plan to use the funds received by the applicant from his property adjustment case to pay out Mr G. Again, the respondent’s attempt to explain this evidence in cross-examination was unpersuasive.
In January 2021, the respondent settled the property adjustment application with Mr G. In return for keeping the Suburb J property, the respondent needed to pay him $46,803.00 and discharge an encumbrance over the property of $92,480.00. The applicant gave evidence that this had long been discussed with the respondent. She was very anxious to retain the Suburb J property.
Mr U held funds for the applicant from his property settlement with Ms F sufficient to assist the respondent to retain Suburb J. Settlement of the Suburb J property took place in January 2021 and the applicant’s funds were used to meet the respondent’s obligations for that settlement. Upon enquiry from Mr U, the applicant said that he did not want to be “placed on the title” to the land “at this time”. There would be stamp duty implications for the parties if the applicant was placed on the title at that time which they wished to avoid. Accordingly, the title to the property was registered in the respondent’s sole name. I accept this evidence. There was no suggestion that the advance from the applicant was a gift to the respondent.
In total, $139,809.47 was paid by the applicant to enable the respondent to receive the Suburb J property. The applicant contends these payments were contributions by him to the respondent’s property in consideration of their anticipated future together. The respondent says that the payments were simply unsolicited gifts from a person with whom she had an agistment agreement.
Ms Y gave evidence. She met the respondent in City Z in Queensland. Ms Y said that the respondent had approached them in 2018 for a loan to help her buy-out Mr G’s interest in Suburb J. She said that the respondent later told her in 2019 that she had met the applicant who had offered her “a gift to pay out her mortgage” and that she was incredulous that someone could be so generous. No particulars of this conversation are given. She subsequently met the applicant in Melbourne when he was in company with the respondent and her children. Her recollection of this meeting was vague at best and she agreed with the proposition that she was not privy to what was happening in the lives of the applicant and the respondent. When pressed in cross-examination Ms Y could not give any particulars of the relevant conversations about the proposed gift (or the prospective loan she and her husband might advance to the respondent).
I found Ms Y’s evidence of no particular benefit. She was unaware of the respondent’s depositions in mid and late 2020 that the applicant was the respondent’s partner. She was unaware of a range of other matters (such as the parties’ engagement for example). It is also inconsistent with the evidence of Mr U, whose evidence I prefer.
I reject the respondent’s claims about these payments. I find that the payments were made by the applicant for the reason he professes.
The applicant also paid the respondent’s legal fees owing to Mr U and her previous lawyer, in a total sum of about $7,500.00.
Mr M returned to build the cabins on the Suburb J property in March 2021. The parties together planned the size and design of the cabins in consultation with Mr M. He observed the same living and sleeping arrangements as he had in early 2020. He would sometimes have coffee with the applicant and the respondent in the morning, perhaps 2 – 3 times each week. They would all discuss the project, and about how things were progressing. Sometimes he had lunch with the parties. He swears that the respondent told him that, “we're engaged”. Mr M said that:
(a) he regularly saw the applicant cooking breakfast for the respondent when he arrived at the Suburb J property during this time;

(b) the applicant was busy on the property, clearing gardens and growing vegetables;

(c) he formed the view from his observations that the applicant was the active person in the relationship in relation to the upkeep of the property;

(d) he was paid by the applicant usually monthly; after providing the applicant with an invoice;

(e) the respondent would direct the design of the projects;

(f) after the first cabin was completed the applicant and the respondent began sleeping and using that cabin, which was set up as a bedroom and in which he saw their “stuff”;

(g) once completed, the second cabin was used by the respondent as a studio; and

(h) he observed that Ms K had moved her sleeping quarters to the loft in the original cottage and Mr L was still sleeping in the cottage.

These observations are consistent with the applicant’s evidence. When the third cabin was completed, it was used by Ms K as her bedroom. Mr L slept in the loft or mezzanine of the cottage.
Mr M said that he was on site at the property building the cabins for about 5 months in total, working an average of 8 – 10 hours per day, Monday to Friday. He summarised his evidence by saying, “Overall, I had witnessed [Mr Melita], [Ms Falvo] and the children living together at the [Suburb J] property over a period of about 18 months (March 2020 to May 2020 and then again in March 2021 to July 2021)”.
Mr M was challenged by the respondent on his evidence, but not effectively. There is no reason not to accept his evidence about all of these matters.
In early May 2021 the applicant supported the respondent by driving her to City AA to see her sister who had discovered a health concern. He stayed with the respondent at her sister’s place in City AA and shared the bed in the spare room with the respondent.
The applicant says that the respondent introduced him as her partner to her sister’s children. I accept his evidence about that. Further, he says that during this trip the respondent did not wear her engagement ring which she took off for safe-keeping. In its place, she wore another ring purchased for her by the applicant.
Sadly for Mr M, the parties did not pay him the whole of his contract price for the work he undertook at Suburb J. He had to sue the parties for satisfaction. Neither defended the proceedings and the respondent did not defend the proceedings on the basis that she was not liable to Mr M. Default judgment was entered against both parties in late 2021.
In about March 2022 the respondent handed in her driving licence and registration plates for two vehicles, one owned by her and the other by the applicant, to the registration and licencing authorities and cancelled their registrations. She placed her own number plate on her vehicle which displayed the word 'Private'. This later led to her being arrested for certain driving offences, the applicant assisting her with her court appearances and her subsequent fleeing to Queensland to avoid a warrant for her arrest.
In about March 2022, the respondent had an accident when she slipped off a step after heavy rain and knocked herself unconscious. The applicant was elsewhere, but the alarm was raised by Ms K. According to the applicant, the respondent blamed him for the accident. The applicant and Ms K nursed the respondent for a few days. She refused to be taken to the hospital and insisted on the applicant remaining with her. She then had the applicant take her to a friend’s house about a week later where she stayed for about a fortnight. During that time, he purchased groceries, bought specific foods and cereal requested by the respondent and delivered it to her. I accept his evidence about these things.
The applicant says that on her return to Suburb J property the respondent was verbally aggressive towards him and told him that she hated him and that she wanted him out of her life. Affection ceased and she became distant.
The applicant says that by the end of May 2022, he had decided to move out. He rented alternative accommodation. However, after about four weeks, he says that the respondent rang him and they started having regular contact again. The applicant did not consider that the relationship was over, and all of his personal property was still in the parties’ bedroom and all of his farm machinery was still at the property.
He says that over the next month, at the respondent’s invitation, he attended the Suburb J property a few days each week doing work around the property. He was invited for dinner and then asked to stay overnight. Sexual intimacy recommenced, and he says, the respondent returned to being loving towards him. They recommenced talking about their relationship and future life together. The respondent asked the applicant to move back in with her. They talked about travelling together to Country S, to Country BB and Country CC. They discussed their shared vision of living off the land with gardens, sheep, being self-reliant and off-grid. They talked about finding ways to diversify their income. They also discussed moving, at some point, to City Z in Queensland together.
By the end of July 2022, the applicant says that he had returned to living full-time at the Suburb J property. They had already recommenced sleeping every night in the same bed and there was regular intimacy.
The respondent was arrested by police in mid-2022 in relation to certain driving offences. She called upon the applicant for support to collect her from the police station. In late 2022 he drove her to the Local Court and accompanied her into the courtroom for a hearing about her charges. Apparently, she left before the charges against her were dealt with and they were dealt with in her absence.
I accept that the applicant provided financial support to the respondent between August 2022 and separation in April 2023. During that period it was her evidence that she was receiving no Centrelink or child support. She was, I find, financially dependent on the applicant. For example:
(a) on 2 August 2022, he caused the proceeds of the sale of livestock in the sum of $10,147.54 to be transferred to the respondent;

(b) he made a payment of $1,000 on 21 November 2022 to the respondent;

(c) he made a payment of $650.00 on 29 December 2022 to the respondent; and

(d) between 23 January 2023 and March 2023, he caused his employer to pay the respondent $5,300.

This is a non-exhaustive list.

The applicant says that in late 2022 he and the respondent became aware that a warrant had been issued for her arrest in relation to the matters dealt with in the Local Court and that the Sherriff had attended the Suburb J property looking for her. Soon thereafter, the respondent left New South Wales and went to Queensland to avoid arrest.
The applicant says that while the respondent was in Queensland, he and Mr L stayed at the Suburb J property and the applicant continued to care for him and work around the property.
The applicant travelled to Queensland on several occasions to spend time with the respondent. In particular, on or around 8 December 2022, he drove with Mr L to Queensland and stayed with the respondent at a property near Town DD. He left the respondent, Ms K and Mr L in Queensland and returned to Suburb J. He returned to Queensland to collect Ms K and Mr L in early January 2023.
After he returned to Suburb J with Ms K and Mr L, the applicant looked after Mr L and took care of the animals on the property. Soon after, however, from some point in January until March 2023, the applicant obtained work in City EE. The applicant and the respondent spoke regularly and the respondent continued to rely upon the applicant for support. For example, at her request, he drove from City EE, collected the children from Suburb J and drove them to see the respondent in Queensland before returning to City EE.
On 4 April 2023 the applicant returned to the Suburb J property. Sometime later the respondent and Ms K arrived. The applicant says that the respondent did not greet him and when she did acknowledge him she was hostile and unfriendly. From that point they no longer shared a bedroom. The following day the respondent moved the applicant’s personal items out of their shared bedroom.
The following day, the respondent was outside of the room the applicant was using as a bedroom and was yelling that she did not want the applicant there any longer. She yelled that she did not love him anymore. The applicant responded by saying that it was his home as well and he did not intend moving anywhere.
The applicant left the property that afternoon to get fuel and when he returned, the respondent had two friends present. The applicant thought it better to leave and so packed some things and left. Many of his possessions remain at the Suburb J property.

The parties were in a de facto relationship

Having regard to the facts set out above, I am satisfied that the parties were living together as a couple on a genuine domestic basis between July 2018 and April 2023. I reach that conclusion for the following reasons.
The parties had a relationship between March 2018 and April 2023. The nature of their relationship changed over time. In my view it became a committed relationship from about June or July 2018 when they commenced spending most of their evenings with one another. I do not find, because I do not think it to be the case, that they shared a common residence at that time. That did not occur until September 2019 when the applicant sold his Suburb H property. But Ms C’s evidence describes the arrangements between the parties, consistently with the applicant’s case:
7. On occasions [Mr Melita] and [Ms Falvo] would come to my home and have a wine, or [Mr Melita] would have a beer with [Mr V], my husband. I recall conversations were both [Mr Melita] and [Ms Falvo] would openly discuss that they, and [Ms Falvo's] two children were living between the [Suburb J] property and [Mr Melita's] property at [Suburb H]. I recall this was in mid 2018. In further conversations [Ms Falvo] and [Mr Melita] told me that [Mr Melita] would move to the [Suburb J] property when his property at [Suburb H] sold.
One needs to be careful about this evidence because on 21 August 2018, after ticking a box on a Centrelink form indicating that the respondent was single, Ms C signed a declaration that, “To the best of my knowledge [Ms Falvo] is maintaining her household with her two youngest children singularly.” This declaration is inconsistent with her evidence in paragraph 7 set out above. But I think it likely that the declaration is false rather than the evidence in the affidavit. Given the obvious friendship that existed between Ms C and the respondent at the time of the declaration, it is likely that she was endeavouring to assist her friend, albeit in a misguided way. That is not to condone Ms C’s conduct, but it recognises the reality of the circumstances. In any event, I would have reached the conclusion I have about the parties spending their time together between their households irrespective of Ms C’s evidence on the point. There is ample other evidence to support that proposition.
To be living together on a genuine domestic basis does not require parties to share a common residence during the relationship (Fairbairn v Radecki [2022] HCA 18; (2022) 275 CLR 400 at [33]). Thus, that the parties did not have a single common residence between June 2018 and September 2019 does not preclude my finding that they were living together as a couple on a genuine domestic basis from June 2018.
From September 2019 they shared the same residence, although I have accepted that the spent most nights together from well before then.
Notwithstanding the short period between May 2022 and July 2022 when the applicant moved away from Suburb J, I consider that they remained committed to each other and living together as a couple on a genuine domestic basis. It is a feature of human relationships that they are challenged from time-to-time by the actions of one or other of the parties and their intensity waxes and wanes. Such events do not necessarily spell the end of the relationship. I do not consider that during the May 2022 – July 2022 period when the applicant lived away from Suburb J, the parties’ de facto relationship ceased. It continued, albeit perhaps with less intensity, but as their reconciliation demonstrated, it endured.
Further, it matters not that there were other periods when they did not live in the same location – for example, when the respondent was at her sister’s home in City AA and when the applicant was working in City EE. Their relationship endured, nonetheless.
Their engagement to marry was demonstrative of their commitment to each other. That they chose to celebrate the engagement with friends and family gives a public recognition to the nature of their relationship and commitment to each other.
The evidence demonstrates financial interdependence. The applicant contributed to the respondent’s welfare and that of her children. He funded improvements to her property to her benefit. The applicant was also involved in the care of the respondent’s children and provided support to them in the ways I have set out above. They plainly intermingled their finances, both on a daily basis for the purchase of necessaries and the discharge of living expenses but also on a grander scale. The applicant met the costs of installation of solar power. He willingly gave the respondent not inconsequential sums of money for her new business, to pay for other improvements to the Suburb J property and to fund her property settlement with Mr G. This level of financial interdependency weighs strongly in favour of the parties pursuing a life together as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis.
Against the proposition that the parties were in de facto relationship and what I have found above was the natural progression of their relationship, the respondent says that from the very beginning, the applicant was not interested in her but only her land. According to her, everything he did was done for the sole purpose of using her land to further what she describes as his farming enterprise and his ruthless pursuit of profit from raising and selling livestock. She gives him no credit for anything he has done to the Suburb J property by way of improvement or the support he gave to her and her children. She asserts that anything that was done, was done by the applicant for his benefit only. His only motive was to steal her property from her by stealth. She submits that save for “a brief romance period” between December 2019 and mid 2020 the parties were never in a romantic relationship and their relationship was purely business.
However, I reject the respondent’s case. It is entirely inconsistent with:
(a) the evidence of Ms D;

(b) the evidence of Ms C;

(c) the evidence of Mr M; and

(d) the findings of fact I have set out above.

The respondent relied upon several witnesses that gave evidence about the public aspects of the parties’ relationship.
Relevantly, Ms FF gave evidence that:
(a) she had never met anyone at the respondent’s place that “she was romantically attached or seeing, or even being introduced to anyone she'd claim to be her partner”;

(b) she described the respondent as a “single mum”;

(c) in 2020 she looked around the respondent’s property and noticed it was in a state of disarray. The respondent told her that “a man she called [Mr Melita], had brought his belongings to it, as apparently he had nowhere else to go after selling his farm”;

(d) “over the years” she had offered to help with the “bare basics” around the house;

(e) “[Mr Melita] was never around, the farming of his cows was most often left to [Ms Falvo] and the kids; and

(f) “She was all alone out there 2020”.

Ms FF also gave evidence of events that she says she witnessed at the time of the parties’ separation in 2023.
I did not find Ms FF’s evidence helpful because it is inconsistent in many respects with the respondent’s own evidence. For example, on the respondent’s case, the parties were in a romantic relationship, albeit for a shorter period than that contended for by the applicant. Her evidence that the applicant was never around is inconsistent with the respondent’s evidence that he was always around and was taking over her farm. Ms FF was not available for cross-examination.
Ms GG gave evidence and was cross-examined. She says that she met the applicant in company with the respondent on one occasion only. She described the respondent as awkward looking, not herself. She did not say why she thought that with any persuasion. She says that she saw nothing whatsoever that would suggest to her “any closeness, intimacy, affectionate association, kissy love stuff between them” that would indicate they were in an intimate relationship. Her impression that day was that they were just “business friends”. She gives no explanation for the applicant’s presence with the respondent, something which would be odd, if they were only “business friends”. She says that she met the applicant more than once, possibly three but more likely two.
The balance of Ms GG’s evidence deals with her observations that the applicant was never at the respondent’s property. On Ms GG’s evidence, she was there regularly because she was a close friend of the respondent and the respondent would share many details of her life with her. According to Ms GG, the respondent only ever referred to the applicant as someone who leased some paddocks from her for his livestock. Somewhat inconsistently with her evidence that she had only met the applicant once, she also says:
I never saw her display any affection towards him that I could possibly construe she was in a defacto. I find it hard to fathom the idea of [Ms Falvo] in a relationship with [Mr Melita]; he was hardly ever around that I saw or, that she never ever talked about him as a boyfriend; that's the kicker: It don't make sense.
I did not find Ms GG a persuasive witness. She was very much an advocate for the respondent and was not, I consider, attempting to give her evidence impartially.
Ms HH also gave evidence. She had not seen the applicant at the respondent’s property on the “several occasions” she had been at the respondent’s property. She does not give evidence about how many times or when that was, although she has only known her since 2022. Ms HH was not available to be cross-examined.
The respondent relied upon an affidavit by Ms JJ. Ms JJ describes herself as practising craniosacral therapist. She was the person that the respondent went to stay with when she fell on the stairs in 2022. She had been treating the respondent for “ongoing stress and trauma as a result of a trespass that occurred at her property in 2021”. She gives evidence of her view that the parties were not in a de facto relationship as a result of her observations and things that she says, in a generalised way, the respondent and her daughter have told her. She does not say what it is that she thinks constitutes a de facto relationship. Ms JJ was not available for cross-examination. Her evidence attracts no weight.

Conclusions

The evidence establishes that the parties lived together as a couple on a genuine domestic basis from about July 2018 until April 2023. Although their living arrangements evolved over time, and notwithstanding periods when they did not share a single residence, the relationship had the hallmarks of a de facto relationship from mid‑2018. From September 2019 they shared a common residence at the Suburb J property, but well before then they spent most nights together and conducted their lives as a couple. Consistently with authority, the absence of a single common residence prior to that time does not preclude a finding that they were living together on a genuine domestic basis.
The relationship endured notwithstanding periods of strain and temporary physical separation, including the applicant’s absence from the Suburb J property between May and July 2022 and other times when work or family circumstances required one or other party to be elsewhere. Those interruptions did not bring the relationship to an end. Rather, the evidence demonstrates continuity, reconciliation and an ongoing mutual commitment. Their engagement to marry, and the public acknowledgment of that engagement, was a powerful indicator of the nature and seriousness of their relationship.
The parties’ financial and domestic interdependence underscores this conclusion. The applicant made substantial contributions to the respondent’s welfare and that of her children, funded significant improvements to the Suburb J property and provided ongoing financial support, including for the respondent’s business and her property settlement. He was involved in the care of the children and the day‑to‑day functioning of the household. The respondent’s contention that the relationship was purely business‑based, or worse an invasion by him of her farm and life without her agreement, is inconsistent with the evidence.

Disposition

Taken as a whole, the evidence compellingly supports the conclusion that the parties were in a de facto relationship within the meaning of s 4AA(1) of the Family Law Act from July 2018 until April 2023.
There will be a declaration accordingly.
Further directions are now necessary for a final disposition of the proceedings.
